ref.title: Parliamentary election reference
ref.lead: Pick a country and an election to see vote shares and seats from ParlGov, then open the same parties in the calculator (shares are renormalized to sum to 100%).
ref.attrib: Source: ParlGov (Parliaments and governments database), development CSV, national parliament elections. Legal thresholds are not embedded—set one manually before opening the calculator.
ref.unavailable: The reference is temporarily unavailable. Please try again later.
ref.thresholdHint: Electoral thresholds are not read from the dataset—enter one manually (percent), as in the calculator.

footer: For learning and analysis—not an official tally or a legal election forecast.

landing.title: Five ways to turn the same votes into seats
landing.lead: Enter your percentages or raw vote totals, hit calculate, and see how Hare, Droop, Sainte-Laguë, D'Hondt, and Imperiali disagree—right in your browser, no sign-up, no cloud storage. Export to Excel when you need a slide or a footnote.
landing.whatP1: Add parties and vote shares in percent (or start from whole vote counts on a separate page), set how many seats you are splitting and the threshold in percent. You get five columns of seat counts plus short explanations of what each rule does.
landing.whatP2: We don’t store your numbers on the server—they stay in your tab until you close it. Handy for a story, a class, or a quick “what if we change the threshold?”.
landing.whoP: Anyone who wants the same inputs compared side by side: reporters, students, teachers, curious voters.
landing.methodsList: Hare and Droop use quotas and remainders; Sainte-Laguë, D'Hondt, and Imperiali hand out seats one by one to whoever currently has the strongest votes-per-seat ratio. Longer notes and links live on the calculator page.

votesForm.title: From raw votes to percentages—automatically
votesForm.lead: Use this page when your sheet says “party—votes”, not “party—percent”.
votesForm.how: Fill in seats, threshold, and each row: name plus vote count (whole numbers, zero or more). We convert votes to shares of the row total and send you to the main calculator to compare methods and export.
votesForm.votesHint: The denominator uses only rows that have both a name and a vote count. Match the aggregation level (district, region, country, etc.) to the territory for which the mandate pool applies.
votesForm.errNeedParty: Add at least one party with a name and vote count.
votesForm.errTotalVotes: The sum of votes must be greater than zero.
votesForm.errMandates: Enter a valid number of mandates (at least 1).

calc.title: Calculator
calc.fromVotesLink: Enter from absolute vote totals (separate form)

hare.title: Hare quota
hare.body:
Quota = valid votes / number of mandates. Each party receives the integer part of votes divided by the quota; remaining mandates go to the largest fractional remainders.

Political science note: fairly proportional; large parties can receive slightly fewer seats than under strongly majoritarian divisor rules.

droop.title: Droop quota
droop.body:
Quota = valid votes / (mandates + 1) + 1, then the same remainder logic as Hare.

Political science note: common in STV contexts; often somewhat less favorable to very small parties than pure Hare.

sl.title: Sainte-Laguë
sl.body:
Sequential allocation with divisors 1, 3, 5, 7, … — each time the highest vote-to-divisor ratio wins a seat.

Political science note: often described as more neutral between large and small parties than D'Hondt; widely used in Nordic settings.

dhondt.title: D'Hondt
dhondt.body:
Sequential allocation with divisors 1, 2, 3, 4, …

Political science note: common across Europe; modestly favors larger parties, which can help coalition stability.

imp.title: Imperiali
imp.body:
Divisors 2, 3, 4, 5, … — same logic as D'Hondt but with a stronger large-party tilt.

Political science note: can under-represent small parties; comparatively rare in practice.
